The Commission's proposal to use frozen Russian assets for reparation loans is a welcome first step. Russian state money must be used for Ukraine's self-defence, not for the aggressor's future.
It also has a sound international legal basis. Reparation loans are legitimate third-party countermeasures to Russia's ongoing war of aggression, which is the gravest breach of the UN Charter.
They meet the requirements of proportionality and temporariness under international law. They also are of an exceptional nature, as applied only in respect of the aggressor and only for the aggression.
Europe has the duty to assist the victim by all possible means. There is no other choice to secure our common European future.

This is our chance to help Ukraine rebuild what Russia destroyed without European taxpayers having to pay a single cent. This is our chance to make sure that Ukraine stands strong in upcoming peace talks. This is our chance to show Putin that Europe has no war fatigue 芒聙聯 that when the US retreats, Europe doubles down.
Opponents of this plan say that it's too risky, that it sets a dangerous precedent if we use money from an aggressive dictatorship. But this is exactly the precedent we need to set. If you don't start a war, destroy millions of homes, bomb hospitals, torture soldiers, kill civilians and abduct children, then you have nothing to fear.

Don't behave like Bolsheviks 芒聙聯 I know you are as far as your attitude towards property is concerned, and you present your Bolshevik attitude very often 芒聙聯 but don't be fools also, because it's foolish. It's not only wrong 芒聙聯 morally wrong, legally wrong 芒聙聯 it's politically stupid!

We must continue to intensify our efforts across all fronts to bolster Ukraine's resilience and strengthen our support. This includes providing additional assistance, remaining committed to Ukraine's European accession path, and seizing every opportunity to bring a swift end to Russia's war of aggression.
To do so, we will continue using all the instruments we have at our disposal. First, we will continue to deliver financial support to Ukraine under the G7 Extraordinary Revenue Acceleration initiative, through which the EU has already disbursed EUR脗聽14脗聽billion this year.
Second, through the Ukraine facility, we will continue to provide significant support to Ukraine as it works to implement crucial reforms.
Thirdly, we will continue to support Ukraine in meeting its defence needs, notably through the Ukraine Support Instrument in the European defence industry programme. Ukraine will be treated as an EU Member State under the instrument and will receive direct support for procurement and ramp-up actions.
Similarly, under the recently established Security Action for Europe (SAFE) instrument, the Member States also expressed interest in involving Ukraine under SAFE.
To address Ukraine's financing needs in 2026, 2027 and beyond, we are working on additional solutions such as the reparation loan. This would provide Ukraine with a stable and predictable source of funding, leveraging the value of immobilised Russian assets while ensuring full compliance with our international law obligations.
Maybe here I'll come to some of the points which were raised during the debate on the support of other partners: we obviously continued engagement with other partners, including our G7 partners, who are providing their share for Ukraine's financing. And it's worth noting that this reparation loan initiative, in a sense, is scalable. So other countries, which also have Russian assets in their jurisdiction can use a similar approach. The UK and Canada have actually signalled their readiness to do so.
Second, on some of the legal aspects, it's worth noting that this proposal stops short of confiscating Russian assets, while at the same time providing much needed financing to Ukraine.
On the arguments for financial stability, it's worth noting that we already took a fundamental decision on immobilising Russian assets in our territory, and it has not led to any financial stability issues or any issues with the international role of the euro.
Also, both the European Council and the G7 have already taken decisions that Russian assets should stay immobilised for as long as Russia does not pay reparations.
So basically, what we are doing with this reparation loan initiative is operationalising these decisions and providing, so to say, for how those cash balances associated with the Russian assets can be used in the meanwhile, so until Russia actually pays reparations.
So once again, I wanted to thank you for your broad support, which was here during this debate. I look forward to continuing engaging with you on this matter in the coming weeks and months. And we certainly must continue to provide our strong support and be very clear and unwavering on this.
Written Statements (Rule 178)

The tragedy and the aftermath have further deepened the polarisation in Serbian society. For the past 11 months, Serbian citizens, led by students, have been protesting across Serbia's towns and cities, calling for accountability for this tragedy. Corruption and the perceived lack of accountability were the main two triggers of protests. Also, the motives for discontent of the people are primarily the lack of democracy, media capture and impunity of politicians.
The Commission has been following the situation in Serbia very closely, as I mentioned in this House during the last debate, not even two months ago. Overall, the police have respected the principle of the freedom of assembly. However, recently several incidents and violence against students, peaceful protesters and journalists have been recorded as well, and there have been instances of suspected excessive use of force by the Serbian police. I wish to reiterate, once again, that we expect police action to be proportionate and to respect fundamental rights. The EU expects these cases to be investigated as a matter of priority. We also condemn all acts of hate, vandalism and violence from protesters and continue to call on all sides to de-escalate tension.
The EU accession process addresses many of the key issues that are at the heart of the debate in Serbia today. As a candidate country, we expect Serbia to respect the key values that are at the foundation of our Union. And as you know, our Union is founded on the values of respect for human dignity, freedom, democracy, equality, the rule of law and respect for human rights. Respect for fundamental rights and values, including freedom of peaceful assembly, media and academic freedom are key elements of Serbia's EU path and need to be adhered to by everyone. The Serbian Government should create an enabling environment for civil society. Intimidation and pressure on civil society is not the way we operate in the Union and must not be tolerated.
As underlined on previous occasions, it is imperative for Serbia to create conditions for a renewed dialogue between stakeholders across the political spectrum and across society. In our view, this should be based on a renewed societal consensus on key reforms, so I welcome the readiness to take it forward. In this context, let me welcome the recent progress made on the law on the unified voter register, and on the council of the regulatory body for electronic media (REM), achieved together with the civil society and the opposition. This comes in addition to the recent creation of a joint monitoring committee for the growth plan, which includes representatives of civil society.
There are concrete first steps towards re-engagement with all parts of the society and across the political spectrum. These agreements have now to be implemented properly, especially the OSCE ODIHR recommendations to ensure a level playing field for all contenders at elections, a long-standing commitment of Serbia's that shall be implemented and respected. A successful election of the new council of media regulator REM, in accordance with the legal requirements, and the new council implementing properly its function, could be the next step. This would serve as an example that progress is possible when there is a will and a constructive approach by all. It will also address an important request of the citizens who protested on the streets.
Dear honourable Members, it is in both Serbia's and our interests to bring a democratic Serbia into the EU. And I want to make it clear once again: I am truly devoted to it. We will continue with our engagement and will do everything possible to help Serbia move forward on its accession path. But as I have already stressed, for this to happen, concrete steps on democratic principles and reforms are needed.
We live in a fractured world 芒聙聯 now is the time for strategic choices. The EU is very clear on where it stands. Serbia needs to make its strategic choice loud and clear too. Close relations with Moscow and Beijing, accompanied with critical statements towards the EU, including relating hostile Russian narratives, is not what is expected from a candidate country. Hostile political rhetoric, including against the EU, its citizens and especially Members of the European Parliament, runs contrary to EU values and standards.
The same goes for disinformation against the EU, spread by some media yesterday and today. Namely, yesterday, the Council adopted its negotiation position on REPowerEU, which includes, for Member States, a ban on importing Russian gas from 2028 onwards. This decision does not imply that EU Member States will have to stop transit of Russian gas to third countries, such as Serbia, so the EU is not cutting any supplies to Serbia. These are lies. Instead 芒聙聯 and I really stress this 芒聙聯 the EU is supporting Serbia, and other West Balkan countries, for diversifying their energy supplies, and for closer integration into the EU's energy networks, both for electricity and gas, as well as through strategic investment. And let me reiterate President Vu脛聧i脛聡's remark in the press conference with President von der Leyen stating Serbia's strategic objective to diversify its gas supplies. Yesterday's decision is fully consistent with this, and the EU is ready to support it.
At the same time, I have to say that we have noted recent progress on Serbia's alignment with the EU's common foreign and security policy, and this approach should be maintained, strengthened and consistently sustained. Much more is needed to reach the CFSP脗聽alignment rate of a country that declares EU membership its strategic goal. We need Serbia to reaffirm its choice clearly and decisively. The EU's doors are wide open and what we have to offer cannot be matched. Serbia's commitment to the EU path can only be proven with its political will to credibly implement the required reforms, and the EU orientation has to be manifested via public narrative and concrete actions, both domestically and externally.
Democratic changes can only come from within the country through internal democratic processes. Citizens call for more rule of law and democratic norms, so the Commission will engage with the competent authorities and other stakeholders to reassure progress on reforms, particularly on the electoral framework, in the judiciary, on the fight against corruption, and media and academic freedom, including completing the election of the new council of media regulator REM.脗聽Now is the moment for the authorities to demonstrate Serbia's commitment to its EU path, both in words and deeds. I look forward to hearing your views on the domestic situation in Serbia.

Serbia's European future remains open, of course, but it depends on respect for our common values. That means an independent judiciary, free media and political pluralism. It is crucial to have much closer alignment with the European Union in foreign and security policy, including towards Russia.
We want Serbia to succeed, of course, but that requires determined reforms, rejection of nationalist narratives from political leaders as well as all sections of civil society, and we have also seen it through the demonstrators. Serbia has made progress, of course, in certain areas, including economic growth, regional infrastructure, connectivity and cooperation with the EU on migration management and energy security. But there are many other fields where there is no progress, not at all.
Our long-standing commitment to Serbia's European perspective is essential, and it always depends on full respect for the rule of law and fundamental rights. Serbia needs a democratic dialogue, de芒聙聭escalation and inclusive political participation. It is Serbia's decision whether it comes back to the European track.

It's in Serbian, but something should sound familiar. That's what the regime in Serbia called the people on the streets: Nazis. Let that sink in.
Do you know that Novi Sad literally means 'new now'? The protesters' wish is simple: a new future. Not tomorrow. Not after another round of no doings. Now.
So now get this grotesque irony, this theatre of power: those ordering the beatings called the beaten 'Nazis'. They invent fake students, '脛聠acis', while the real students bleed for democracy. Oh, and don't rush with those textbooks you promised, because your lesson by force is over. History will grade you.
(The speaker spoke in a non-official language).
We will see soon enough.
I choose justice, I choose Novi Sad, new and now.

Thank you, Commissioner脗聽Kos. Thank you, because it was time that we hear the right word from the European Commission. We stand by this commitment. We stand by the European students. And we stand by the European future of the Serbian people.

It is precisely Serbia's EU path that offers solutions to many of the issues that are at the heart of the discussion in Serbia today, notably in the field of the rule of law. The people in Serbia deserve a European future, and the perspective of EU membership offers the transformation of societies in a comprehensive and sustainable way, based on democratic values and principles.
We will continue with our strong engagement with the competent Serbian authorities, parties across the political spectrum and civil society to ensure that it remains on its EU path and overcomes the current political impasse. Trust among all stakeholders has to be restored, but also trust in the EU. We have seen the first steps in this direction in the past days, and we will support Serbia to continue on this path.
Today's debate has once again shown the importance of adherence to EU values in our candidate countries. This is, and will, remain at the heart of the EU accession process. The EU budget is an investment budget designed to provide the necessary resources to all European citizens, who can benefit from common action. Experts agree in saying that the EU budget is a crucial tool for a robust and stronger Europe, and this is also recognised by our citizens, who rightfully expect more and more from the EU budget. The latest Eurobarometer 2025 survey highlights Europeans' expectations for security, unity and more resources to address global challenges. In the face of geopolitical uncertainty, citizens across the Union are calling for stronger protection and greater solidarity.
This is exactly what Parliament's position is about. Back in April, Parliament adopted a strong set of guidelines for the financial year ahead of us, underlining the importance of strategic preparedness and security, as well as economic competitiveness and resilience. We made it clear that we expect the 2026 budget to provide people in the European Union with a robust ecosystem to deliver on their priorities in a prosperous Europe.
Since then, the Commission submitted its draft budget. Unfortunately, it does not come as a great surprise to the European Parliament that the Council cuts the Commission's proposal. The Council proposed to make unjustified cuts across a series of programmes 芒聙聯 I would like to say flagship programmes 芒聙聯 without proper justification. We oppose all these cuts. If it is expected from the European Union to enhance its action, it is not acceptable to cut the funding of the Union where it is needed.
Overall, we support the draft budget of the European Commission and, at Parliament, we have looked in a very responsible way at areas where funding has proven insufficient and more is needed. We are deeply aware of the critical situation of the Union budget, which is a victim of its success and provides for too limited availabilities compared to all the challenges ahead.
This is why our position is a reasonable one, which only proposes essential and targeted increases. I would like to underline a few priorities.
We need to support our businesses, especially small and medium enterprises and our researchers who work for a better future in Europe. Therefore, we propose to reinforce Horizon Europe and the Connected Europe Facility 芒聙聯 some of our most successful programmes.
We need to ensure the protection of our citizens, so we also confirm Parliament's strong support to further strengthen the Union Civil Protection Mechanism. Agriculture is another key pillar of our Union. Our farmers 芒聙聯 and particularly young farmers 芒聙聯 need continued support for the promotion of their products and to maintain profitability. This is also a widely shared priority in this House.
Security is another core concern of EU citizens, along with the need to strengthen our defence capabilities. We propose to reinforce key programmes in these areas, namely on asylum, migration, border management and military mobility.
As we are living in a global world, we are directly affected by what is happening outside our borders, including the continued illegal war of aggression by Russia in Ukraine. For this reason, we put forward essential increases of funds allocated to our southern and eastern neighbourhood, as well as humanitarian aid.
Dear colleagues, we have a strong support from several groups. I would like to emphasise our very good cooperation with the shadow rapporteurs. We achieved this majority on the committee level. Now I expect that it will be adopted by the whole Parliament and I am looking forward to the cooperation during our negotiations also with the European Commission and with the Council.

This is an important moment for the Parliament where we set our policy priorities, and how to finance them.脗聽Our common approach reflects our common values. In addition, it sets the course for the future of Europe as we see it.脗聽We are of course part of the globalised world, with many ongoing conflicts, including on our borders.脗聽We are also exposed to geopolitical events. We also have to respond to the urgent challenges, including cyber security.
In the last years, the EU adopted several ground-breaking legislative initiatives, such as the Digital Services Act, the Digital Market Act, or the Artificial intelligence Act, just to name some of them.
Consequently, we tasked the EU institutions to implement these initiatives and monitor their application.脗聽But to be able to do that, the EU institutions must be able to do their job. And for them to be able to do so, we must give them sufficient resources.脗聽If we can talk the talk, but do not walk the walk, we put all our institutions in a difficult situation. And the Union at risk.
Dear colleagues, our key priorities are reflected in our budget proposal.
And considering the difficult overall financial situation of the EU budget, we are being very responsible in planning it.脗聽And I believe that our proposal demonstrates just that. Our position is very focused, very reasonable and targeted to real needs.
However, certain difference between our institutions remain.脗聽But we have a common task to deliver for our citizens.脗聽It is them we serve. Not ourselves.
As the rapporteur for other sections, my message is clear.脗聽We must enable our institutions to have enough staff to deal with their increased tasks and responsibilities, and mandatory expenses.脗聽But also they need to be able to protect themselves from the new realities, such as cybersecurity issues.脗聽More efficient and safe EU institutions are in our common interest.
So, dear Minister脗聽Wammen, dear Commissioner Serafin,脗聽I am sure we will be able to find a common ground that will be based on the real needs for each of our institutions.脗聽They are the backbone of the EU, working for our citizens, but also the Member States.脗聽And that the result in the end will be a good one.
I look forward to today芒聙聶s debate and I thank you for your attention. Thank you also to my colleagues.

We live in difficult times, and, therefore, we must prioritise and ensure that the budget for 2026 allows the Union to act effectively, without unnecessarily increasing the pressure on national budgets and tax payers. We need margins and flexibility for the EU budget to cope with war, crisis and challenges, notably Ukraine and the Middle East, but also the unforeseen ones. If almost all margins and special instruments are consumed, how can we then guarantee the flexibility to cope with unforeseen challenges?
Dear colleagues, the Council would not be able to approve all the European Parliament's amendments, should you vote them through tomorrow. In addition, the Council remains fully committed to the agreement on the fifty-fifty split for EURI interest costs that was reached last year. With this in mind, I find it difficult to understand the idea from Parliament of excluding liquidity costs from the fifty-fifty benchmark. These costs have been a part of the interest cost since the very beginning.
To conclude, we share many priorities, but we also have serious work to do in the coming weeks. Dear colleagues, tonight I reaffirm that the Council's willingness to reach a balanced agreement, and I am sure that the Commission will assist us in finding sound and reasonable compromises. Let me, therefore, conclude by expressing my sincere hope that by working together, we will be able to find the best answers for Europe. And the good news is, we have done it together before, and I am convinced we can do it together again.

It is not surprising that at this point of the budgetary process, the position of the European Parliament and the position of the Council diverge from each other substantially. In a week, we will start the conciliation and hopefully these divergences will start to shrink.
But today, let me thank you for the amendments proposed by the Parliament that reinstate the expenditure at the level proposed in the draft budget by the European Commission. I have been clear and consistent that the Commission does not consider the Council's cost cuts as adequately justified.
Of course, I also note that the Parliament proposes reinforcements in a number of programmes across all headings that go on top of the draft budget. We will need to treat such reinforcements very cautiously. The remaining financial resources are limited and we will have to prioritise. We must focus on programs where meaningful adjustments can be made and concessions on both sides will be necessary.
We should remember that reinforcements in this year's budget will compromise our capacity to respond to foreseeable and, more importantly, unforeseeable needs in the budget for 2027. We have to preserve some budgetary space to avoid a risk of cuts in the spending programs in the last year of the current Multiannual Financial Framework. I urge you to consider this issue with utmost prudence.
I would like to ask the Parliament and the Council to consider the amending letter of the European Commission as a starting point in the negotiating process. That letter addresses some concerns of both institutions, in particular the payment appropriations, agriculture and the EU Juri interest line. I'm confident that the negotiations will continue in a positive spirit, and that the Parliament and the Council will manage to reconcile their positions.脗聽As actually was mentioned also by Minister Wammen, that experience is already there.
Let me assure you again that I and my team will spare no effort to support your work throughout the conciliation to reach a good compromise.

The 2026 statement of estimates of the Court of Justice of the European Union requests the creation of 10 new permanent posts to meet Cybersecurity Regulation obligations. This is a budget in which young farmers, young students through the Erasmus programme, the Connecting Europe Facility, Frontex, Eurojust, Ukraine, our neighbourhood, military mobility receive solid support. This is a position that deserves to be supported. On behalf of the EPP Group, we are going to support the work done by our two rapporteurs and we are confident that they will receive a clear majority tomorrow, where we will see pro-European groups united.
They make the most with very limited resources and we are now going to, together, decide a budget for a year, which is close to the end of this Multiannual Financial Framework, and we can already see the consequences of some of the mistakes which we made when we designed this Multiannual Financial Framework.
We should not repeat these mistakes again for the next Multiannual Financial Framework. And the mistakes were: firstly, this budget was insufficient, there were not enough margins, not enough reserves, it could not react in situations of crisis. We see also the margins very limited for 2026, so we need to draw the lessons for the future there.脗聽Secondly, we did together NextGenerationEU. We see reimbursing NextGenerationEU costs money. The correct lesson to be drawn is we need to work together on the introduction of own resources.
And, Minister, if we all want Europe to do more, particularly on competitiveness, on security, on defence, that cannot be done at the expense of the traditional policies. We as a Parliament will defend, also for the next MFF, an ambitious but future-oriented common agricultural policy and cohesion policy. What I want to say is we cannot do more with less. This is something that we have to take into account also for the next MFF.

This budget starts translating political solidarity into concrete financial decisions. It acknowledges the disproportionate burden carried by the eastern border Member States and reinforces our collective resilience. And I would like to thank the rapporteur, Andrzej Halicki, and my colleagues in the Budgets Committee, in particular those who visited the Latgale border region on a special mission this April.
The resolution signals a paradigm shift. This House is finally accepting that EU funds can, and must, be spent on border and security, and we see enhanced support for the Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund. This, in its turn, strengthens the migration pact, providing essential tools for the return procedures of irregular migrants. This is vital for regions like Latgale which are confronting weaponised migration head on.
We see critical increase in investment in military mobility, which, among other things, may help my home country to complete the Rail Baltica project. This is a strong start, but as we look toward the 2027 budget and the new MFF, we must go further. Border infrastructure is vital, yet we must equally support the ordinary Europeans, municipalities and businesses in the border regions, who are absorbing the social and economic fallout of the war.
Finally, we cannot preach collective resilience while maintaining a hypocritical discrimination against farmers in central and eastern Europe. The next MFF must guarantee equal support for all farmers across the Union.

At the same time, European citizens struggle to make a living. Do Europeans really need more beautiful EU agencies and EU action plans? Instead of feeding this bloated bureaucracy, we should focus on serious topics, not ideological experiments. And most importantly: taking action now.
If you need more green or leftist workshops, please pay for them yourself. Let's bring discipline, responsibility and realism back to this Union. The taxpayer's duty is not to fund every Brussels fantasy.

The role of the Commission is to facilitate the upcoming negotiations, and we will play that role. In that spirit, we will present an assessment of the amendments proposed by the Parliament, and of the Council's position. We will have an opportunity to discuss it on 4 November when we meet in the format of the Conciliation Committee.
As I have said before, I am convinced that the key to successful negotiations lies in both institutions setting clear priorities. Bearing in mind that the financial resources in the next year's budget are very limited. Let me assure you that the Commission will act as an honest broker. My services, and me personally, we will do our utmost to prepare the ground for good and successful negotiations.

I would like to thank the Members of the European Parliament for their help in casting light on the European Parliament's priorities. We share the vision of a strong Europe. At the same time, we also have disagreements in terms of the size of the budget, but not when it comes to the importance of Europe.
I have noted that most of the suggestions tonight were on increasing expenditure. But, dear colleagues, at the end of the day there will be limits to the budget. Not everything can be equally important. We have to prioritise. That is not a job only for the Council, or the Commission, or the European Parliament 芒聙聯 it is a job for all of us. We will need to find common ground. As I said in my initial remarks, I am confident and optimistic that we will do so, because I know that the spirit of this House, the spirit of the Commission and the spirit of the Council is that that is our job, because we have a job to do for the citizens of Europe.
Therefore, the Council will do its utmost to engage in a constructive dialogue, allowing us to come to a balanced agreement on the budget for 2026. Therefore, I am looking forward to this process, and I am encouraged by both the interventions by the rapporteurs and by the Commission.

So I would like to emphasise, once again, that we cannot accept the cuts, especially on flagship programmes like Erasmus+ or Horizon. We would like to have top-ups on very important issues, including EU4Health. We have a new committee and this is the first step for a better future, of course initially. It's the first time we have support for external border protection, including in the budget, support so widely in Parliament 芒聙聯 do not ignore that this is the first time.
So I think that we are close to also having satisfied conciliation and the negotiations, including understanding from both sides 芒聙聯 the European Commission and and the Council. But we have to be united and strong tomorrow. So please vote in favour of this resolution and the proposal.

Dear colleagues and shadow rapporteurs, I want to thank you for the very constructive work and cooperation so far. I believe tomorrow we will get strong support for the Parliament's position ahead of the conciliation talks.脗聽I look very much forward to these discussions and I am hopeful we will find an agreement that will be a good one.
Le vote aura lieu demain.

Honourable Members, 25 years ago, the first EU-Africa summit took place, in Cairo, actually, and now we are preparing the seventh EU-Africa summit on 24 and 25 November in Angola. Over this quarter of a century, we have been building strong relations between the European Union and Africa, also thanks to the active participation of the European Parliament and the Pan-African Parliament.
At a time of geopolitical competition, Africa and Europe have a shared interest in dialogue and also in mutual beneficial cooperation. Marking the partnership's 25th anniversary, 2025 is a crucial year, I would say, for the EU-Africa relations, with increased engagement that will culminate in the mentioned summit in November.
We are definitely Africa's prime partner. The European Union remains Africa's first trading partner, first investor by far, first partner on peace and also security, and first official development assistance provider and humanitarian donor. EU trade with Africa accounted for 33脗聽% of Africa's total trade. We have a positive agenda converging with many of the objectives of the African Union's Agenda 2063. So, the European Union is constantly adapting its relations with Africa at regional levels, but also at national levels, and the joint vision for 2030 adopted by the European Union and African Union Heads of State or Government at the last summit, in 2022, is actually guiding our partnership. The recently adopted pact for the Mediterranean will also contribute to the EU-Africa relations.
The EU partnership with Africa includes four key areas: its prosperity, its peace and security, its migration and mobility, and multilateralism. First of all, the prosperity part: the Global Gateway Africa-Europe Investment Package targets at least EUR脗聽150 billion of investments in Africa by 2027, and we are well on track to reach this target. Global Gateway is a very ambitious investment strategy and also evidence of Team Europe working together. It is more than an offer; it is also a choice: a choice of partners, a choice of values, of principles, of a world vision, of sustainable long term development.
The second point, peace and security: since 2021, the European Union has supported African partners with over EUR脗聽1 billion through the European Peace Facility (EPF). Around 70脗聽% of all EPF support 芒聙聯 taking Ukraine aside, of course 芒聙聯 is dedicated to African partners. The European Union has also 12 civilian and military missions in Africa, and this represents a European commitment of some 2脗聽000 soldiers, police and civil servants providing advice and support to African counterparts, and the European Union also strongly supports African peace support operations.
The third point, migration, key to me, of course, especially: and when managing migration, we work on preventing, of course, irregular migration, also combating migrant-smuggling and trafficking in human beings and improving effective, safe and also timely returns, but also readmission, of course, and also sustainable reintegration. And at the same time, we work on developing and also expanding pathways for legal migration, for regular migration, and also mobility of students and researchers.
And the fourth point, the last point, the multilateralism point: we have a very ambitious and also forward-looking partnership firmly rooted in a multilateral system, with the UN at its core, and we want to deliver a strong message on the respect of the rules-based international order and the commitment to work together in international fora. I am very much convinced that Europe and Africa working together on global challenges will have a definite effect on the 21st century.
So, the parliamentary dimension of the EU-AU partnership is a vital pillar that strengthens our political and also our democratic foundations, and parliaments altogether represent the voice of the people, of course, and through interparliamentary dialogue, we ensure that the partnerships also reflect not only the will of governments, but also the aspirations and concerns of our citizens. Regular engagement between the European Parliament and the Pan-African Parliament allows for deeper understanding, deeper mutual understanding, more transparency and earned accountability and, as demonstrated by the European Parliament, a strong parliament makes for a strong union.
We are very much confident that the existing partnership and the support provided by the European Union to the Pan-African Parliament will also further strengthen its role and its overall contribution to continental integration and to the EU-AU partnership.

Some might think, when they think about Africa, in all its diversity, it would be about development, and development is important. Some might think it would be about migration, and migration is important. Some might also see it's about youth 芒聙聯 strong youth 芒聙聯 in Africa, striving for prospects. Some might think it is about business partnerships, and I want to clearly emphasise and underline that these things are connected. Actually, when it comes to development, time and again I quote Ronald Reagan, who said, 'The best social programme is a job'. And the same is true for development 芒聙聯 the best development aid is economic partnership.
This is why Global Gateway is of the utmost importance in our EU-Africa partnership, but also when it comes to other parts of the world, to try to influence the rest of the world under the umbrella of other values than the values we strive for. Then,脗聽time and again, I underline 芒聙聯 also to our African partners 芒聙聯 that it's the EU that is trustworthy, it's the EU that's rule-of-law based, and it's the EU that's reliable in the longer term. And this is why partners in the EU and partners in Africa belong together for the better of the population of both.

Ten eerste is er het belang van uitwisseling tussen mensen, elkaar leren kennen en samenwerken, vooral jonge mensen die in Afrika gaan studeren of jonge mensen die in Europa komen studeren. The EU is the largest trading and investment power in Africa. These policies should be aligned for more effective external action.
